The 3.4 release with my attempt at a fix for this issue was seriously broken. The .decoder attribute was a lambda function and thus would get bound with 'self' when called. As a result, 3.4 was completely unusable. My apologies for that. I never ran the tests, which would have caught this issue. Releasing with running the tests is unforgivable.

I've taken another stab at allowing customization. The default behavior will still be to attempt decoding using UTF-8.

Consumers that require no decoding for the bytes coming in on the stream  (strict RFC compatibility), it's very easy to globally configure the irc library to simply pass through bytes directly. Simply set ServerConnection.buffer_class to LineBuffer (which no longer does any decoding).

If the consumer wishes to change the decoding behavior (to use a different encoding or handle multiple encodings or otherwise wrap the lines as they're processed), simply replace ServerConnection.buffer_class with another compatible class.

I've released this new approach as 3.4.1 and ran the tests this time.

I appreciate your suggestions and patience on this matter. Please let me know if this approach will be suitable.

I think this would break the gabbihtml handler (https://github.com/cdent/gabbihtml/blob/master/gabbihtml/handler.py#L97), which uses `gen_replacer()` and not `replacer()` and it assumes that only the last response will be handled.

a) At the moment my technique for dealing with this is change the test such that it is a diagnostic, same request info but change status to whatever I'm seeing in real life and check only response_strings with a nonsense value. This will get the error message to show the response body.

b) Obviously this is less than ideal. One potentialy solution would be to change verbose:

* continue to accept `true` to do what it currently does
* also accept other values which signal different levels of verbosity

I'm not sure what would be the best values. Some options for comparison and discussion (no preference on my part should be construed from these representations they are just ideas to move the brain meat):

```yaml
verbose:
    - headers
    - body
```

```yaml
verbose: 2  # or 1, 1 means headers, 2 means headers + body
```

I'm sure there are additional options, such as ways to control if verbose applies to both request and response or only one.

I'd hate to see this get out of hand though. I guess it could also be possible to just turn on full verbose, but the reason I didn't do that is that would get noisy with long bodies (especially binary ones) and I didn't want to get into this situation where only some content-types were visible.

(blah, blah all the usual rules about yeah, good idea, needs to be simple and not noisy and not break the fundamental ambiance)

Bearing in mind that the concept of country is fuzzy and doesn't always match the concept of locale (eg: what geographical area exactly is `it_CH`), the datasets are meant to represent a statistaclly realistic set of people living in a Country.

That means that a name shouldn't be included just because someone famous lives there. Ideally, we'd like every list to be from an official governmental statistical office (ie: from a census)

Don't take unsourced providers as a model to follow. Historically, list of names in `Faker` providers were not always based on official statistical data. But we've changed that approach and now we require that every new update must reference some source: https://github.com/joke2k/faker/blob/master/docs/coding_style.rst#data-sets . 

If anybody wants to update the `nl_NL` provider, they will have to get their list from some offical source, and reference that source in the provider.

Minimap2 is properly behaving. The root of the problem is you are using a wrong reference genome. The "toplevel" mouse genome is ~8GB in size. It is so large because it includes alternate haplotypes with sequences identical to the primary assembly hard masked as "N". For this 8GB genome, minimap2 will create a multi-part index. With your change, you will see SQ lines inside the alignment section. Samtools will report an error.

You should use "primary_assembly" instead. "Top_level" assembly is worse for almost everything. If I were Ensembl, I wouldn't put this confusing file on FTP.

No worries! As for contributing, most definitely! There are three main areas:

1) Implementing sections of the Zendesk API that are not yet supported.

2) Writing tests (or coming up with a testing strategy even). I'm not very good at testing and have implemented a few strange tests, however the vast majority of the wrapper is untested.

3) Figuring out a new way to generate documentation from the source code. In the early days I wrote some code for Sphinx to generate docs (https://github.com/facetoe/zenpy/blob/master/docs/api_doc_gen.py). This worked well until the wrapper's structure changed and now it doesn't really work anymore. The main problem is that there are a lot of nested callable classes and I'm not sure of the best way to present this. For example, with the ChatApi, you have a callable class that contains other callable classes as attributes, so you can do things like: `zenpy_client.chats()` or `zenpy_client.chats.agents()`, or `zenpy_client.chats.agents.me()`. Another example is the `UserApi` which contains the `UserIdentityApi` in the `identitites` attribute. Hi Brian,

This behaviour is pretty normal: `@wraps` replaces the original function with another one, copying the name and docstring. The signature is different. It's easy to fix though specifically for this decorator in Python 3.x (not sure about the `x` though):
```diff
diff --git a/argh/utils.py b/argh/utils.py
index 8650bb7..c960863 100644
--- a/argh/utils.py
+++ b/argh/utils.py
@@ -49,6 +49,8 @@ def get_arg_spec(function):
     arguments of instance methods (`self`) and static methods (usually `cls`
     or something like this).
     """
+    if hasattr(function, '__wrapped__'):
+        function = function.__wrapped__
     spec = compat.getargspec(function)
     if inspect.ismethod(function):
         spec = spec._replace(args=spec.args[1:])

```

Tbh, I don't even understand why the example on the landing page works because it doesn't use literal markers.

I had a short look into the code base, and the type checking is problematic.

Take [this](https://github.com/cocagne/txdbus/blob/master/txdbus/marshal.py#L434) line:

```python
    if not isinstance(var, six.string_types):
raise MarshallingError('Required string. Received: ' + repr(var))
```

The problem is, on Python 2 this will always evaluate to `False`! For both bytes and unicode strings.

```python
(cpy361_7) oberstet@thinkpad-t430s:~/scm/crossbario/crossbar-fabric-device-agent$ python2
Python 2.7.12 (default, Nov 19 2016, 06:48:10) 
[GCC 5.4.0 20160609] on linux2
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> import six
>>> six.text_type
<type 'unicode'>
>>> six.binary_type
<type 'str'>
>>> type(b'hello') == six.binary_type
True
>>> type(b'hello') == six.text_type
False
>>> six.string_types
(<type 'basestring'>,)
>>> isinstance(u'hello', six.string_types)
True
>>> isinstance(b'hello', six.string_types)
True
>>> 
(cpy361_7) oberstet@thinkpad-t430s:~/scm/crossbario/crossbar-fabric-device-agent$ python
Python 3.6.1 (default, Apr  2 2017, 18:19:20) 
[GCC 5.4.0 20160609] on linux
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> import six
>>> six.text_type
<class 'str'>
>>> six.binary_type
<class 'bytes'>
>>> type(b'hello') == six.binary_type
True
>>> type(b'hello') == six.text_type
False
>>> six.string_types
(<class 'str'>,)
>>> isinstance(u'hello', six.string_types)
True
>>> isinstance(b'hello', six.string_types)
False
>>> 
```

The reason is, that on Python 2, both str (bytes) and unicode derive of basestring (https://pythonhosted.org/six/#six.string_types, https://docs.python.org/2/library/functions.html#basestring).

The right way to check is via `six.text_type` and `six.binary_type`. See above.

There are a lot of ways to compile C++ code.  Dlib tries to be convenient and let you do it any way you want.  The recommendations are only a small set of the possible ways you could do it.  

But if you aren't familiar with different C++ build methods you should just type exactly what the instructions say to do to run the examples.

> As an aside, you really should avoid building lib files in visual studio.
> A much less error prone way to build C++ applications is to compile
> everything together in the same project, as the examples included with dlib
> show.

I am so far in the opposite mindset of this response that you would never get me to see your reasoning behind this so I plan to  just ignore this issue. :)

> I wouldn't install them to the same folder.  That's asking for ODR
> violations.

I am not sure what you are referencing about installing to the same folder? The includes or the libs?



If you run a cmake build now on the current cmakelists file, at the end of the compile you will end up with this

/installfolder/include/dlib
/installfolder/lib/
/installfolder/share/doc/

in /installfolder/include/dlib is the config.h file along with all other includes.
in /installfolder/lib/ is dlib.lib (release) and dlibd.lib (debug)

Normally from this point with a new project using dlib, I would just add /installfolder/include to the include search directories and /installfolder/lib to the lib search directories. Then I would compile for debug linking to dlibd.lib and release linking to dlib.lib.

If I do the above using dlib built without DLIB_ENABLE_ASSERTS during dlib's cmake build I end up with a config.h file like this:

```
...
/* #undef ENABLE_ASSERTS */
#define DLIB_DISABLE_ASSERTS // asserts always disabled 
...
```

If I then compile in debug mode I will get a linking error caused by the ENABLE_ASSERTS not being defined in the config.h but the library being built with it on. If I build with DLIB_ENABLE_ASSERTS on during the cmake build I get the following config.h:
```
...
#define ENABLE_ASSERTS  // asserts always enabled 
/* #undef DLIB_DISABLE_ASSERTS*/
...
```
and everything builds correctly (both debug and release). But I am forced to have the release version with ENABLE_ASSERTS on.

Now if I fix it with your suggestion above (adding DLIB_ENABLE_ASSERTS ON to the if (NOT CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_DEBUG MATCHES "-DENABLE_ASSERTS"). Then ENABLE_ASSERTS will be always on for both debug and release as the if statement will always happen. The CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_DEBUG check is high in the file and unless someone manual changes it somewhere else it will never have -DENABLE_ASSERTS in it. And once DLIB_ENABLE_ASSERTS is set ON for any reason it will be on for both the debug and release builds of dlib.

For me the fact that ENABLE_ASSERTS is forced on for debug build really means it not an config option any more so hard coding a pre-processor in the config file does not seem that odd. It seems like the following would work for all cases:

```
#ifdef NDEBUG
#cmakedefine ENABLE_ASSERTS       // asserts always enabled 
#cmakedefine DLIB_DISABLE_ASSERTS // asserts always disabled 
#else
#define ENABLE_ASSERTS // asserts always enabled for debug builds
#undef DLIB_DISABLE_ASSERTS
#endif
```

I've updated the patch to hopefully address all your concerns above.  To keep things clean it turned into a larger patch than originally intended, but I think this way it is still relatively easy to read and it is extendable because the connection handling for each protocol is isolated from the others.  

* The StatsClient is still thread safe because the tcp connection socket is kept in a `threading.local` object.
* The Pipeline should also be fine and still work the same way
* By default `fail_silently` is `True` so the behavior of the old version doesn't change unless the user passes `fail_silently=False`.
* There are no loops or waits anymore. It only tries to reconnect once if a connection has once been established, but has died in the meantime. If this reconnect fails it will swallow the error by default or raise an exception if `fail_silently=False`. Basically this means that whenever `send()` gets called on an instance that has `fail_silently` off, it should be wrapped in a `try-except` to catch `socket.error`s.

I have not yet updated the tests or the docs yet, but if the patch itself looks acceptable I can go ahead and do that also.  Look forward to hearing your feedback.

No worries, looking forward to hearing your feedback.  We are assuming there are other potential users of pystatsd who could find applications for a TCP transport, which is why we'd like to contribute back if we can.  Should it not be a fit we can always write a custom statsd client for our needs and maintain it in-house.  In the meantime we'll go ahead with work on a patch that addresses the points you brought up earlier and we can see where things end up.

I meant of course #1053.

For example, before, for given query logic was:

````
select 1 as a into #test
select * from #test
update #test set a = 2
select * from #test
````

1. we call `execute`, which calls `find_result_or_done`
3. `find_result_or_done` finds DONE token for first line: `select 1 as a into #test` and returns False, `execute` ignores result
3. because `find_result_or_done` exited before it found RESULT token, we have no description and no resultset
4. we call `nextset`, which calls `find_result_or_done`, it finds RESULT token for second line: `select * from #test` and returns True
5. we can correctly fetch all rows
6. we call `nextset` again, which calls `find_result_or_done`, it finds DONE token for third line: `update #test set a = 2` and returns False, which is passed to user
7. `nextset` returned False, we stop processing even though there is one more resultset

Hey, that was a puzzle for me! Spent 2 hours, while expected 15 minutes issue. So, what is going on here.

In psutil/tests/__init__.py there is create_sockets(), which calls unix_socketpair(). The problem is
that unix_socketpair() has nothing to do with UNIX socketpair(2) syscall, and this is very confusing.
Real socketpair(2) syscall creates two sockets, that are connected. Neither is a server and neither
is client. They are just connected. What unix_socketpair() does: it creates listening socket, it
creates other socket (name it client), it connects client to the server socket, but it doesn't accept
the connection on the server side. So what happens in the kernel is that 3 sockets exist: server
listening socket, client socket and a newborn socket hanging on the accept queue of the server.
The newborn socket isn't yet known to the process. The process must call accept(2) to get a file
descriptor of that socket. And, yes, the file descriptor for it doesn't yet exist. So, we have a valid
UNIX socket without file descriptor. So, when we grab 'kern.files' sysctl, and then we grab
'net.local.stream.pcblist', in the latter we have a socket that shall not have a match in the
array obtained in 'kern.files', and this is correct.

What can be done here? Either you need to remove the unix_socketpair() function and use
socket.socketpair() Python function. That may not work on Windows. Either you need to accept
the socket in the unix_socketpair(), and make it return 3 sockets: (server, client, accepted).
And in this case better rename it from unix_socketpair() to something else.

Of course if a provider doesn't **ever** send the proper "year" it shouldn't be added for movies.

My concern is more about the way providers individually send the year information. Some might always send it, some not always. As a result we will be priviledging one provider over the other. Maybe a provider will send the correct year for our movie (but we don't know, it's not in the filename) but it will score less than a provider that didn't send the year at all.

Treating them equally forces subliminal to use other means to do the matching and not rely on the year anymore.

Also, if you don't know the year of the movie, you can't seriously expect subtitles to be correct unless you have a hash match.

You're mistaken in your interpretation of that sentence, it won't autodetect anything, it will just return bytes if no encoding is specified, unicode otherwise. Here is a quick example:

```python
>>> import codecs
>>> open('test.srt', 'wb').write('ééé'.encode('latin-1'))  # a file with latin-1 encoding
>>> codecs.open('test.srt').read()
b'\xe9\xe9\xe9'  # plain bytes here, no auto detection
>>> codecs.open('test.srt', encoding='latin-1').read()
'ééé'  # now we have string
```

The code itself is OK. (It could use some polish, but it is at least functional.) However, much like with #67, I want to take a moment to step back and think about design. In this library, we have [`gen_choice`](http://fauxfactory.readthedocs.org/en/latest/api.html#fauxfactory.gen_choice), which acts much like [`random.choice`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/random.html#random.choice). However, we have no methods that provide sampling of multiple items, either with or with out replacement. [`random.sample`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/random.html#random.sample) provides sampling without replacement, but there is no standard lib function that provides sampling with replacement.

So - I understand that this function is intended as a replacement for [`pick`](https://github.com/RedHatQE/cfme_tests/blob/master/utils/randomness.py#L41). But what's the more general problem being solved here?

* Is the goal sampling without replacement? If so, `random.sample` provides that already. There's a case to be made for adding `gen_sample` to fauxfactory.
* Is the goal is sampling with replacement? If so, a good solution might be a simple function like this: `def gen_choices(choices, n): return [random.choice(choices) for _ in range(n)]`
* If the goal is something even more exotic? Can you clearly outline that goal?

You should be able to accomplish this with the `.bumpversion.cfg` file.

Create `project/.bumpversion.cfg`
```ini
[bumpversion]
commit = True
tag = True
current_version = 0.0.1

[bumpversion:file:setup.py]
```

e.g. `project/setup.py`
```python
#!/usr/bin/env python

from distutils.core import setup

setup(name='Example',
      version='0.0.1',
      description='Bumpversion',
      author='John Doe',
      author_email='John.Doe@python.net',
      packages=['distutils', 'distutils.command'],
     )
```

Now issuing `bumpversion patch` in your project root will detect and bump `0.0.1 -->  0.0.2` accordingly.

> I'd like to extend this to support both adding new transports and removing existing ones, from outside this loop.

It's not clear to me what you mean by outside the loop. Either you want to affect the sub-streamers list at streamer runtime and in this case you could indeed use the `StreamerManager` within a custom operator (which is what `base_combine` is doing). Hi @akhilman, thanks for creating this issue.

I'm not convinced those operators are worth being exposed though, since `map` already picks one or the other depending on the provided function. I can only think of two use cases where `map` is not enough:

**1.** A coroutine function is provided, but it's not meant to be awaited right away:

```python
async def _await(arg):
    return await arg

xs = stream.iterate([1, 2, 3])
ys = stream.combine.smap(xs, asyncio.sleep)
zs = stream.map(ys, _await)
await zs  # wait 6 seconds
```
This feels weird and unlikely.

**2.** The provided function is not a coroutine function but it's meant to be awaited anyway:

```python
xs = stream.iterate([1, 2, 3])
ys = stream.combine.amap(xs, lambda x: asyncio.sleep(x, x))
zs = stream.print(ys)
await zs  # wait 6 seconds and print 1 2 3
```

This seems more useful, although there are other ways to achieve the same purpose like defining the coroutine function explicitly:

```python
async def corofn(x):
    await asyncio.sleep(x, x)
[...]
ys = stream.map(xs, corofn)
```
Or use some kind of `_async` helper:
```python
def _async(fn):
    @functools.wraps(fn)
    async def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
        return await fn(*args, **kwargs)
    return wrapper
[...]
ys = stream.map(xs, _async(lambda x: asyncio.sleep(x, x)))
```
What do you think?

hmmm...that's an interesting use case. Did you know that the file `some_file` was a "plain text" file from the outset? If so, you can either rename the file to `some_file.txt` and use textract in the usual way `textract.process("some_file.txt")`.

This isn't documented, but you could also do something like this if you'd rather not rename the file

```
from textract.parsers.txt_parser import Parser

parser = Parser()
text = parser.process("path/to/file/without/an/extension")
```

If this is a more general problem, I could imagine a few ways of extending textract to address this issue. The first would be to allow users to specify an extension when the call textract with something like `textract.process("some_file", extension="txt")`. 

This requires a user to know the extension up front though, which is kind of the entire point of using textract in the first place. Another option would be to have a fallback method of determining the file type based on the output of the `file` unix command. If the filename extension exists on the filename, then we use that as the default. Huh, ```len(r'\c\n') == 5```? It is always 4 here. But maybe you meant ```len(r'\\c\n') == 5```?

Anyway, per STOMP 1.2 rules, I don't think (92, 99, 10) should escape to (92, 99, 92, 110), because when unescaping that back, it becomes (58, 10): 92+99 transforms to 58, and 92+110 to 10. So an encode-decode roundtrip would turn ```r'\c\n'``` into ```':\n'```.

Instead, (92, 99, 10) should escape to (92, 92, 99, 92, 110) which unescapes back to the original.

I think there's a bit of a bug in the STOMP spec's value encoding chapter: it says carriage return, line feed, or colon should be escaped, but backslash should also be in that list.

Also, I now also believe that ```stomp.utils.parse_headers()``` is broken: decoding a value should be done in one pass, instead of running the replacement operations all over the entire string in succession. (It also doesn't pay proper attention to the protocol version in use. Neither do the places where PREAMBLE_END_RE and LINE_END_RE are used, but that's another issue.) I'll open separate PRs or issues for these later, let's not delve into them in this one :)

He maintains a list of intents that have been visited on the session, and a switch statement interrogates where in the sequence he is, which gives the current intent context in case it's mapped to one or more flows. 

The example he gives is "Yes" or "No", which could mean different things depending on where you are in the conversation. With Flask-Ask you'd do something like

```
@ask.intent("YesIntent")
def yes():
   visited_intents = session.attributes.get('intent_sequence')
   last_intent = visited_intents[-1]
   if last_intent == 'foo':
      # process YesIntent in the context of 'foo'
      ...
   elif last_intent == 'bar':
      # process YesIntent in the context of 'bar'
      ...
```

@alinahazan, thank you for the detailed issue report.

1. I'll probably need to see the debug-level output to know what's going on here, specifically the output related to Trusted Advisor. The easiest way to get that would probably be to just run ``awslimitchecker -r REGION_NAME -l -vv`` directly and post the output.
2. When was the last time you refreshed the Trusted Advisor checks through the AWS Consule? Support for doing that through awslimitchecker was only introduced in [0.7.0](https://github.com/jantman/awslimitchecker/blob/master/CHANGES.rst#070-2017-01-15), and it appears your wrapper script is not making use of that.
3. Can you please tell me what region you're seeing this problem in?

As per the [documentation on supported EC2 limits](http://awslimitchecker.readthedocs.io/en/latest/limits.html#ec2), the ``Running On-Demand r3.2xlarge instances`` limit is one of the many limits that are not currently exposed via an EC2 API; they can either be retrieved via Trusted Advisor, or need to be manually specified. 20 is the default value.

I can think of a few reasons why you might be seeing this behavior:

1. Trusted Advisor isn't really active for your account.
2. Trusted Advisor isn't reporting on that limit for that region. I'm not sure if TA supports all limits in all regions or not...
3. Your Trusted Advisor Service Limits check is stale. You can either use the command line or [python options](http://awslimitchecker.readthedocs.io/en/latest/python_usage.html#refreshing-trusted-advisor-check-results) to refresh it using awslimitchecker >= 0.7.0 (see the [docs section on Trusted Advisor](http://awslimitchecker.readthedocs.io/en/latest/internals.html#trusted-advisor)), or you can log in to the AWS Console and view the Trusted Advisor -> Performance -> Service Limits check manually; viewing it should cause it to refresh.

If it's not possible to get the updated limit value via Trusted Advisor, then your only option is to specify your limit value manually as a [Limit Override](http://awslimitchecker.readthedocs.io/en/latest/internals.html#limit-value-precedence). For command-line usage, see [Overriding Limits](http://awslimitchecker.readthedocs.io/en/latest/cli_usage.html#overriding-limits). For Python usage, see [Setting a Limit Override](http://awslimitchecker.readthedocs.io/en/latest/python_usage.html#setting-a-limit-override), as well as the AwsLimitChecker class's [set_limit_override()](http://awslimitchecker.readthedocs.io/en/latest/awslimitchecker.checker.html#awslimitchecker.checker.AwsLimitChecker.set_limit_override) and [set_limit_overrides()](http://awslimitchecker.readthedocs.io/en/latest/awslimitchecker.checker.html#awslimitchecker.checker.AwsLimitChecker.set_limit_overrides) methods. There is a more detailed example of how to set limit and threshold overrides from Python in the [Full Jenkins Example](https://github.com/jantman/awslimitchecker/blob/master/docs/examples/check_aws_limits.py).

-Jason

> One possible, but performance terrible, approach that we could take would be to wrap the file in a decoder, and also an encoder in some acceptable encoding (utf-8), which then would get fed into the underlying csv.reader.

Turns out that the Python 2 csv module actually has doing that as an example at the end of the documentation. https://docs.python.org/2/library/csv.html#examples

I've now written a pure-python backport of the Python 3 csv module, so we could choose that approach to solving the problem. It would just be using the same code as the Python 3 version of unicodecsv when we detected an encoding other than `ascii` or `utf-8`.

But because the implementation is in pure python, I think it could likely be slower than the special encoding wrapper to ensure that csv was always dealing with `utf-8` bytes.

@jdunck : I'm interested in writing up a solution to this problem, but I'm not sure which approach would be better. Is it better to use the decoder, or to use the backport?

@jwrdegoede 
> I'm not sure what you mean with the older more complex way

I mean this beast: http://libusb.sourceforge.net/api-1.0/mtasync.html#threadwait

This is what I implemented in this python wrapper, as it describes that having a handler loop in a thread and a synchronous USB call (say, a bulk read), the handler loop could "steal" the completion event and let the synchronous call wait for an event which will the never arrive (I didn't check the code, and maybe I misunderstood the doc - or mis-remember it now as I did not re-read it entirely). Such issue does not exist at all if one only uses the async API.

About thread-safety, thankfully (despite the Giant Interpreter Lock not being too popular) python takes care of this for us, so that's not a problem here.

@jhoenicke 
> The main thread hangs in controlWrite, while the event was handled by the event handler thread.

Good idea to use ltrace/strace.

Could you try building libusb from @jwrdegoede 's repository and try to reproduce with it, as he reported there is a known deadlock regression in 1.0.20 ? python-libusb1 honours OS's library lookup order: LD_LIBRARY_PATH for linux, something similar but with another name for OSX, and I think windows looks in the current directory before going to C:\Windows\System32 (or whatever it is nowadays).

There has been a change in the fallbacks logic over hvad versions indeed. Here is a short history:

  * First implementation used a wrapper to automate subqueries. Automation was a nice feature, but queries with many results generated many queries (roughly 1 additional query for every 100 rows).
  * Then hvad 0.5 introduced a better way to handle fallbacks, using a custom JOIN clause, drastically reducing the number of queries. That what `SelfJoinFallbackQuerySet` is about.
  * After manager evolutions in hvad 1.1, the features from `SelfJoinFallbackQuerySet` were merged into the main manager. The API was also clarified to the way it currently is.

So here is today's state:
  * `MyModel.objects.untranslated()` is intended for getting untranslated instances. Without fallbacks. As a matter of fact, ̀.unstranslated().use_fallbacks()` is not supported on Django 1.9 and newer.
  * For fetching translated instances, use `.language()`.
  * If fallbacks are wished for, simply add `.fallbacks()` after `.language()`.

For instance:
```python
# Load object without any translation
untranslated = Normal.objects.untranslated().get(pk=42)
untranslated.some_translated_field    # will try to load translation from the database

# Load object in English. Will fail if object is not available in English
translated = Normal.objects.language('en').get(pk=42)

# Load object in English. Will fallback to French, then Serbian, then whatever is available.
translated = Normal.objects.language('en').fallbacks('fr', 'sr').get(pk=42)
```

If you have translation-aware code, you may also be interested in the possibility of loading all translations as a queryset:
```python
all_translations = Normal.objects.language('all').filter(pk=42)

# You can then do, for instance:
as_dict = dict((item.language_code, item) for item in all_translations)
as_dict['en']   # will return the English version of the object, if there is one
```
This returns a queryset with as many copies of the object as there are available translations, each copy being loaded with a translation. Keep in mind copies are independent, changing an untranslatable field on one copy will not update the other copies, it's mainly intended for manipulating translations.

I hope it helps, feel free to ask if you need any help.

I see. I think I understand now. Each client has a single event queue. Events from the queue are processed one after another. If one event takes very long, then the events that come after it will be delayed. Sounds like this is the problem you wanted to avoid.

For the eventlet/gevent servers, I've added the `async_handlers=True` option when you create the `Server` object, which basically runs all event handlers in separate threads. This can sometimes cause race conditions and other problems, but on the other side, allows any handler to run for as long as it wants, and other events are processed regardless of that.

def print_summary(symbol, shape=None, line_length=120, positions=[.44, .64, .74, 1.]):
    """Convert symbol for detail information.

    Parameters
    ----------
    symbol: Symbol
        Symbol to be visualized.
    shape: dict
        A dict of shapes, str->shape (tuple), given input shapes.
    line_length: int
        Rotal length of printed lines
    positions: list
        Relative or absolute positions of log elements in each line.
    Returns
    ------
    None
    """
    if not isinstance(symbol, Symbol):
        raise TypeError("symbol must be Symbol")
    show_shape = False
    if shape is not None:
        show_shape = True
        interals = symbol.get_internals()
        _, out_shapes, _ = interals.infer_shape(**shape)
        if out_shapes is None:
            raise ValueError("Input shape is incomplete")
        shape_dict = dict(zip(interals.list_outputs(), out_shapes))
    conf = json.loads(symbol.tojson())
    nodes = conf["nodes"]
    heads = set(conf["heads"][0])
    if positions[-1] <= 1:
        positions = [int(line_length * p) for p in positions]
    # header names for the different log elements
    to_display = ['Layer (type)', 'Output Shape', 'Param #', 'Previous Layer']
    def print_row(fields, positions):
        """Print format row.

        Parameters
        ----------
        fields: list
            Information field.
        positions: list
            Field length ratio.
        Returns
        ------
        None
        """
        line = ''
        for i, field in enumerate(fields):
            line += str(field)
            line = line[:positions[i]]
            line += ' ' * (positions[i] - len(line))
        print(line)
    print('_' * line_length)
    print_row(to_display, positions)
    print('=' * line_length)
    def print_layer_summary(node, out_shape):
        """print layer information

        Parameters
        ----------
        node: dict
            Node information.
        out_shape: dict
            Node shape information.
        Returns
        ------
            Node total parameters.
        """
        op = node["op"]
        pre_node = []
        pre_filter = 0
        if op != "null":
            inputs = node["inputs"]
            for item in inputs:
                input_node = nodes[item[0]]
                input_name = input_node["name"]
                if input_node["op"] != "null" or item[0] in heads:
                    # add precede
                    pre_node.append(input_name)
                    if show_shape:
                        if input_node["op"] != "null":
                            key = input_name + "_output"
                        else:
                            key = input_name
                        if key in shape_dict:
                            shape = shape_dict[key][1:]
                            pre_filter = pre_filter + int(shape[0])
        cur_param = 0
        if op == 'Convolution':
            if ("no_bias" in node["attrs"]) and (isinstance(node["attrs"]["no_bias"],(bool,int)) and int(node["attrs"]["no_bias"])) or ((isinstance(node["attrs"]["no_bias"],(str)) and bool(node["attrs"]["no_bias"]))):
                cur_param = pre_filter * int(node["attrs"]["num_filter"])
                for k in _str2tuple(node["attrs"]["kernel"]):
                    cur_param *= int(k)
            else:
                cur_param = pre_filter * int(node["attrs"]["num_filter"])
                for k in _str2tuple(node["attrs"]["kernel"]):
                    cur_param *= int(k)
                cur_param += int(node["attrs"]["num_filter"])
        elif op == 'FullyConnected':
            if ("no_bias" in node["attrs"]) and int(node["attrs"]["no_bias"]):
                cur_param = pre_filter * (int(node["attrs"]["num_hidden"]))
            else:
                cur_param = (pre_filter+1) * (int(node["attrs"]["num_hidden"]))
        elif op == 'BatchNorm':
            key = node["name"] + "_output"
            if show_shape:
                num_filter = shape_dict[key][1]
                cur_param = int(num_filter) * 2
        if not pre_node:
            first_connection = ''
        else:
            first_connection = pre_node[0]
        fields = [node['name'] + '(' + op + ')',
                  "x".join([str(x) for x in out_shape]),
                  cur_param,
                  first_connection]
        print_row(fields, positions)
        if len(pre_node) > 1:
            for i in range(1, len(pre_node)):
                fields = ['', '', '', pre_node[i]]
                print_row(fields, positions)
        return cur_param
    total_params = 0
    for i, node in enumerate(nodes):
        out_shape = []
        op = node["op"]
        if op == "null" and i > 0:
            continue
        if op != "null" or i in heads:
            if show_shape:
                if op != "null":
                    key = node["name"] + "_output"
                else:
                    key = node["name"]
                if key in shape_dict:
                    out_shape = shape_dict[key][1:]
        total_params += print_layer_summary(nodes[i], out_shape)
        if i == len(nodes) - 1:
            print('=' * line_length)
        else:
            print('_' * line_length)
    print('Total params: %s' % total_params)
    print('_' * line_length)

The intent is that no one should really be thinking about `watch-del` at all, because critical components in our infra do not have long running connections to watchman (eg: mercurial and git fsmonitor extensions).  With that in mind, there is no way to reference count watches safely.  Instead, watchman will age out and retire idle watches after about a week (this is configurable).

Tools and automation should not be running `watch-del` unilaterally because they will break the performance of other consumers by causing watchman to recrawl, which can take several minutes on loaded systems with very large repos.   (One exception to this is for CI: when the CI job is finished, it makes sense for it to cancel the watches because it totally owns the isolated build environment, and won't mess things up for other consumers)

`watch-del` is considered to be a troubleshooting command rather than a common workflow command.

There is a insane number of layers of caching to work around when trying it out:

- bundle (enable dev-mode for plone and plone-logged-in resources)
- the js (reload http://localhost:8080/Plone/++resource++mockup/structure/js/views/tablerow.js in the browser or disable browser-cache)
- the xml-file (reload http://localhost:8080/Plone/++resource++mockup/structure/templates/tablerow.xml  in the browser or disable browser-cache)
- the mo-file (restart the instance to recompile it)
- the widgets-locales (localStorage.clear() in the browser-console takes care of that) 

Forget one of these and you'll not see the current state fo the code.

Re: detours, I also agree it's too easy and a bit strange to leave _file-explorer mode_ once you click a non-folderish item. However I wouldn't redirect to Edit mode as you proposed. I think that's based on the belief it's less expensive and less riskier to get to `View` view by accident than to `Edit` view. Thanks! That's a really neat way of getting around django-compressor's "only one output file" restriction.

It's just a shame about the need for a hard-coded `LIBSASS_SOURCEMAP_PREFIX` path - it seems to make this a lot less useful than it could be. From what I can see, it prevents you from having stylesheets at different paths relative to `STATIC_URL` (e.g. `static/css/layouts/news.scss` and `static/css/components/carousel.scss`), or `@import` lines that import from other apps.

I think it ought to be possible to eliminate the `LIBSASS_SOURCEMAP_PREFIX` setting, because the Django config already contains all the necessary information to convert between filesystem paths and URLs. We could probably do it by matching `INCLUDE_PATHS` in reverse - for each file path listed in the source map, expand it into an absolute path then look for an entry in `INCLUDE_PATHS` which is a prefix of that path. The remainder can then be passed to `django_static` to generate a URL.

        reply= self.remote_pull(self.pullSocket)

    def sell_order(self, symbol, stop_loss, take_profit):
        self.buy= "TRADE|OPEN|1|"+ str(symbol)+"|"+str(stop_loss)+"|"+str(take_profit)
        self.remote_send(self.reqSocket, self.buy)
        reply= self.remote_pull(self.pullSocket)
        return reply

        self.remote_send(self.reqSocket, self.close_buy)
        reply= self.remote_pull(self.pullSocket)
        return reply

In luigi land, only workers ask for work. Typically, you have workers being launched at least hourly and then everything not finished will be run and that satisfies most business requirements. Furthermore there might already be workers that have registered that task and they will run the tasker even sooner if the central scheduler permits (check the configuration section for retrying). Having some delay is in general a good practice as theirs not point in constantly restart something that fails and waste resources (remember, luigi was originally made for Big Data).

If you for whatever reason really want to restart the task immediately, configure your scheduler appropriately and make your callback start a new worker (either grammatically with `interface.py` or start a new `luigi` cli process).

While I feel that being able to inject your own http adapter is something that we want to add support for, there is a couple implications that other members of the boto org will need to decide upon because as of now we hardly expose anything (except maybe timeouts) about our http layer:

1) Do we want to expose custom http layer abstractions through the creation of the client or somewhere else like on the session or through an event? I bring this up because I prefer that we do not want to bloat the client signature. I would prefer, if we want to inject http adapters through the client constructor, to put it in the client config since we already have timeout configuration there.

2) How does this affect botocore's relation with ``requests``? I know we have had discussions about moving off of ``requests`` and possibly create our own http layer as we already patch a bunch of parts of ``requests`` and every time we vendor in a new version of ``requests`` there is something that we need to change in the botocore code to avoid breaking changes. Taking in this change would not allow us to move off of ``requests``. Furthermore if we do more patching/upgrading of ``requests``, it may have an unknown affects on custom http adapters users may have provided and we would have to be very careful about this.

I think we (members of the boto org) need to make decisions on some of the points that I brought up before we can move forward with this pull request.  This may take some time depending on what we decided. Also @pfreixes feel free to chime in on any of the points I brought up.

There is a really huge difference between the reset and the reboot functions,

**Reboot**:

> Issues a command to the guest operating system asking it to perform a reboot

**Reset**:

> Resets power on this virtual machine. If the current state is poweredOn, then this method first performs powerOff(hard)

Just like in the real world, if you want to turn off your computer you will click the "Shut Down" button in Windows/Linux  - thats mean **Reboot**.

But if you will press the button on your computer case for a couple of seconds until it power off immediatly - thats mean **Reset** (Try not to do that at all - bad for PC),

```
import re
import time
import robot
from robot.api import logger
import selenium.common.exceptions as exceptions
from Selenium2Library import utils
from Selenium2Library.keywords.keywordgroup import KeywordGroup
from Selenium2Library.keywords import _WaitingKeywords


class SeleniumExceptionMatcher(object):

    def __init__(self, user_repr):
        exclass, _, regexp = user_repr.partition('|')
        if not hasattr(exceptions, exclass):
            raise ValueError("selenium.common.exceptions does not define %s" % exclass)
        self._exception = getattr(exceptions, exclass)
        if regexp:
            try:
                self._regexp = re.compile(regexp)
            except Exception, e:
                raise ValueError("Bad regular expression '%s': %s" % (regexp, e))
        else:
            self._regexp = None

    def matches(self, ex):
        if not isinstance(ex, self._exception):
            return False
        if self._regexp:
            if not self._regexp.search(ex.msg):
                logger.debug("Pattern '%s' not found in exception message '%s'" \
                             % (self._regexp.pattern, ex.msg))
                return False
        return True

    def user_repr(self):
        repr = self._exception.__name__
        if self._regexp:
            repr += '|' + self._regexp.pattern
        return repr


class _WaitingKeywordsMonkeyPatches(KeywordGroup):

    def __init__(self): # no init before patching
        # key:browser instance, value:SeleniumExceptionMatcher[]
        self._exception_matchers = {}

    def set_ignored_wait_exceptions(self, *exceptions):
        """Sets the exceptions to ignore while waiting.

        `exceptions` must be exception class names defined in 
        selenium.common.exceptions. The exceptions will be ignored by the Wait 
        Until * keywords in Selenium2Library for the _current_ browser 
        instance. For more control a regular expression can be appended to 
        the exception class after a pipe. When a regular expression is 
        specified, an instance will only be ignored if the exception message 
        matches the regular expression. The same exception can appear multiple 
        times. 

        Example:
        | @{old ignored}=                  | Set Ignored Wait Exceptions | InvalidSelectorException | WebDriverException|modal |
        | Wait Until Page Contains Element | id=name                     |                           |                          |
        | Set Ignored Wait Exceptions      | @{old ignored}              | # restore previous        |                          |
        """
        browser = self._current_browser()
        old_ex_matchers = self._exception_matchers.get(browser) or []
        ex_matchers = [SeleniumExceptionMatcher(ex) for ex in exceptions]
        self._exception_matchers[browser] = ex_matchers
        return [em.user_repr() for em in old_ex_matchers]

    def _wait_until_no_error_ignoring(self, timeout, wait_func, *args):
        timeout = robot.utils.timestr_to_secs(timeout) if timeout is not None else self._timeout_in_secs
        maxtime = time.time() + timeout
        matchers = self._exception_matchers.get(self._current_browser()) or []
        ignored_names = []
        while True:
            try:
                timeout_error = wait_func(*args)
                if not timeout_error: return
            except exceptions.WebDriverException, e:
                ignored = False
                for ex_matcher in matchers:
                    if ex_matcher.matches(e):
                        ignored = True
                        name = e.__class__.__name__
                        # log warn level event at most once per exception type per wait
                        if name not in ignored_names:
                            ignored_names.append(name)
                            self._info("Ignored during wait: %s: %s" % (name, e))
                        else:
                            self._debug("Ignored during wait: %s: %s" % (name, e))
                        break
                if not ignored:
                    raise
            if time.time() > maxtime:
                raise AssertionError(timeout_error)
            time.sleep(0.2)

    _WaitingKeywords.__init__ = __init__
    _WaitingKeywords.set_ignored_wait_exceptions = set_ignored_wait_exceptions
    _WaitingKeywords._wait_until_no_error = _wait_until_no_error_ignoring
```

Francesc,  I think your reminder was a good idea because I did find a few days to work on it. I've gone through numexpr and converted it from using strings and characters in the virtual machine to 4-byte words passed into C by Python bytearrays. This is a fairly substantial refactor of the code (1000 + 3000 lines changed).  I've put up a branch (4byte_word) on my GitHub:

https://github.com/robbmcleod/numexpr/commit/f0efb84152b3b3f9200f7e095cec918376a537af

This is just an intermediate push to see what I've done.

These changes mean now we have 4 billion op-code addresses instead of 127, so it should be possible (although very laborious) to implement the entirety of numpy's functionality into numexpr.  Similarly the maximum number of registers has also been expanded, from 255 to 4 billion.  

Types are now encoded by kind and byte order, so np.float32 -> 'f8' and np.complex128 -> 'c16', etc, instead of a single character. All signatures are passed as tuples (or lists) of strings instead of just strings. Casting is currently controlled by a tuple of (kind,byteorder) but some things will have to change to mimic numpy casting. Many debug strings are output, I couldn't really figure out how to attach a debugger, so speed will be poor and output messy.

I've tested certain simple cases and it mimics numpy results.  However, the testing suite result in a lot of errors, which at first glance mostly result from casting rules being different from numpy.  Float and complex types seems to work universally from what I've hand tested, ints sometimes break (i.e. division), and strings are broken ATM (casting problem). 

Right now I just have bool, int32, int64, float32, float64, complex64, complex128, and strings implemented. The implementation of int byte order in C was incorrect, I think this is possibly a legacy issue from the 32-bit era.  

I'm thinking about how to generate opcodes.hpp and funccodes.hpp programatically to avoid a mass of typing to implement all the smaller and larger byteorder dtypes, and unsigned ints. I wonder if we need funccodes.hpp or if it can be rolled into opcodes.hpp? 

Python built-in types (long, int, float) are a bit of a PITA because they are platform and version dependant.  Right now they are being cast to their numpy homolog. I'm unclear why people would use numpy ndarrays of native types, and therefore if this is a problem or not?

I haven't tested the code for endian-proofness yet (it works on Linux), or 32-bit operation, or any Python other than 2.7.  This will probably just entail some #define statements.  

Nothing has been tested related to PyTables.

I'm going to take a break from the code for the moment to think about the issues. Basic tasks going forward:

1.) Find and implement numpy-like casting rules.
2.) Add other dtypes in a sensible (preferably procedural) fashion.
3.) Fix bugs that I've mistakenly introduced.
4.) Add more functions.  I'm especially interested in linking FFTW, as I currently use a mix of pyFFTW and NumExpr.
5.) Test build with the Intel compiler.

Well the idea of raising the error is, people using qtpy are encouraged to write code that looks like qt5 code, so if the have qt4 installed and try to access a method that is only available on qt4, it should raise an error, so they know which method to use.


```python
from qtpy.QtWidgets import SomeWidget

# User with qt4 installed
>>> SomeWidget.oldMethodOnlyOnQt4()
Exception: oldMethodOnlyOnQt4 only available on Qt4, use newMethodOnQt5.

# User with qt5 installed
>>> SomeWidget.newMethodOnQt5()
:-)
```

As I thought about this further I think the best way for us to visualize this is to plot just these > +/-2 standard deviation daily returns as a continuous equity curve.  So basically just setting equal to 0 all returns that fall in between +/- 2 standard deviations, and then doing cum_return(all_tail_returns)

so by looking at this next to the overall equity curve of the algo we can visualize whether all of the tail returns are clustered near each other (e.g. like SP500 tail returns would be clustered in 2008/2009), as well as whether the negative or positive tail returns happen to to be auto-correlated with one another, etc.

Yes, I do think you are correct, the problem is that normalizing the data throws everything out of whack when shorting is involved. We can't use the absolute value of the short positions and still have it make sense. 

Consider a portfolio with 1x leverage that is equally long and short. They sell 50% of the account value and get the cash proceeds, then buy assets with the other 50%. That means the account has 100% of the account value sitting in cash still, but some of it is collateral for the short sales. That cash earns interest and is an important component of a long/short strategy, especially in a high interest environment.

I we lose that when normalizing the long/short/cash components because they should equal 200% of the account value if using absolute values. 100% in cash, then 50/50 in long/short stocks.

Having this return the dataframe defined on line 59 before the normalizing would be more correct IMO. I don't think the area plot is the best thing for this either. 

Here are a few plots of a long/short strategy without normalizing the data, the area plot is not very nice, but the density plot, box plot, and histogram capture the distribution of allocations nicely.

In the meantime, I've placed my notes into devel/modules/CGNotes.tex as suggested by @rmjarvis , and also added a section attempting to push the Bernstein&Gruen interpolation equations through the Wiener-Khinchin theorem to see if I could robustly derive the (co-)variance of interpolated Fourier amplitudes given a discretely sampled auto-covariance function and asserted real/Fourier interpolation kernels.  The results of that (assuming I did the math right), is that even if the discrete version of image noise is stationary, and can hence be described by a discrete covariance function / power spectrum, a continuously interpolated noise image will possess non-stationary noise, or equivalently, that the interpolated Fourier amplitudes are correlated.  In hindsight, I think this makes sense, since, for instance, I would expect the variance of interpolated samples to always be less than the variance of the samples themselves.  I'm not sure if this helps or not with determining the "right" way to propagate correlated noise, however.

> should we allow in-place division of two integer Images?

I think `image_int /= 2` is worth enabling, and it seems that numpy does allow this in place without any trouble, so that's good.  I can imagine cases where people would expect to be able to divide an integer image by a scalar in place, where the result would just round (or truncate, really) to an integer.

I can't think of a use case for `image_int1 /= image_int2`, but it feels a bit inconsistent to allow in-place division by a scalar, but not allow this.  So I guess I'm inclined to fix it so this works (which I know how to do).

Il serait possible temporairement de les mettre dans un autre fichier et de faire un legislation_at ... new.xml dans les formules... 
Mais ce n'est pas possible je crois pour les barèmes des cotsoc, qui vont faire appel à des paramètres formatés, qui passent notamment par preprocessing.py.

C'est une solution qui peut marcher. Mais c'est une n-ième couche de complexité. Et la séparation des paramètres par grand domaine (cotsoc.xml etc) est perdue.

Discussions : 
[Ici](https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-france/commit/319ee16b7064926d170faa0ac23ea9e62a64cf82#commitcomment-16532690) et [là](https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-france/commit/dfebad0761f41f8c79d76c58cf46cd77c72fb70d#commitcomment-16532727)

Nous venons d'en discuter cet après-midi.

Tout d'abord le point d'entrée [`fields`](https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-web-api/blob/next/docs/endpoints.md#fields) est "deprecated". La raison est qu'il avait été conçu spécifiquement pour le [démonstrateur OpenFisca](http://ui.openfisca.fr/). Modifier les champs proposés pour un simulateur donné revient à modifier cette UI, et inversement; ce qui n'est pas souhaité.

Donc nous conseillons d'utiliser le point d'entrée [`variables`](https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-web-api/blob/next/docs/endpoints.md#variables), qui exporte la liste complète des variables d'OpenFisca.

À la charge du développeur de choisir les champs qu'il affiche. En l'occurence plusieurs salaires sont retournés par le point d'entrée `variables`, le salaire net, le salaire de base, le salaire imposable. Sachant que pour calculer l'IRPP tu peux utiliser le salaire imposable, libre à toi de l'afficher sous forme de champ de saisie.

En ce qui concerne les catégories (columns tree), sachant qu'il s'agit d'une catégorisation des variables d'entrée réalisée à la main, nous pourrons soit le proposer au téléchargement sous forme de JSON statique, soit tagger chaque variable avec un nom de catégorie (à décider).

Dans cette configuration nous n'avons plus besoin d'activer la réforme sur l'inversion de revenus, car elle ne sert qu'à obtenir le salaire de base de manière approximative, mais celui-ci n'intervient pas dans le calcul de l'impôt, mais des prestations sociales.
En d'autres termes si tu ne demandes pas le calcul du revenu disponible, cela devrait convenir.

I understand the urge to have function to merge font tables that are in two or more fonts, but that is a s completely different function than otf2otc. The point of a TTC file is that it is, at a high level, simply several different fonts stuck together in a single file, with the bonus that you only need one copy of tables that are identical.  otf2otc identifies identical or non-identical OpenType tables without any understanding of what is in the data, and sticks them together.  It is by design simple, stupid, and fast. The additional functionality you want is fundamentally different: take several different fonts, and merge their data into a single font.  It does not make sense to me to have the hugely complex function of merging two font files into one pushed into the subsequent step of building an OTC; the former should be a separate program. 

Such programs do exist. The only OpenSource program (that I know of) which does merge several fonts into one font is indeed mergeFonts, referenced earlier in this thread. I would never attempt to develop such a program for the AFDKO without dire need and serious funding, and would in any case then put my effort into extending 'mergeFonts' instead.

Hi @tom-haines, this happens to be a distinct issue from the one above.  I've opened an issue (GoogleCloudPlatform/gcloud-java#1014) to track fixing it.  The issue here is that the IP address for the Docker container isn't recognized as a local or loopback socket, so `gcloud-java` considers it a "project endpoint" rather than a local host.  This means two things:
1) You should use scheme in the host URL (in 0.1.7, scheme is not allowed for local host, but is required for project endpoints).
2) The version of the `gcd` script you're running (and the version that gcloud-java uses right now) uses a slightly different URL construction than the actual Google Cloud Datastore URL.  It requires an extra "/datastore" (e.g. `http://<hostname>:<port>/datastore`).  This is going away in the next version of the local emulator. `gcloud-java` plans to add a fix so that your Docker example (provided you add scheme to the host URL) should work.  This fix depends on both this library's client being updated and the next version of `gcloud-java` being released.

If you need a quick workaround to upgrade `gcloud-java` and communicate with `gcd.sh` running in a container, use the URL `http://<hostname or IP>:<port>/datastore` instead of `<hostname>:<port>` when constructing `DatastoreOptions`.  Keep in mind you'll have to change the URL again to `http://<hostname or IP>:<port>` when you update to the future release of gcloud-java that contains the fix for GoogleCloudPlatform/gcloud-java#1014.

If you look at this [snippet](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSCloudFormation/latest/UserGuide/quickref-ec2.html) you will see: ```"SecurityGroups" : [{ "Ref" : "Ec2SecurityGroup" }],```. This is usually expressed in troposphere as an argument ```SecurityGroups=[Ref(Ec2SecurityGroup),]```. But that is because a Ref is occurring (meaning you're probably creating it in the CloudFormation script). But that is really a list of strings so you could use ```SecurityGroups=["moonshot",]``` for a pre-exising SG.

How you get a name or id (depending on vpc or not) into your script is up to you. You can hard code it, pull it out of AWS via boto dynamically, or even use a parameter to allow it to be included into your script upon launch.

Note, if you do that latter using a parameter, you need to so the Ref trick again. Slight tweaks to the EC2InstanceSample.py:
```
...
sg_param = template.add_parameter(Parameter(
    "SecurityGroup",
    Description="Existing Security Group",
    Type="String",
))
...
SecurityGroups=[sg_param],
...
```

No idea what is wrong with your other script. It seems to imply you have a parameter object being created which you're trying to call. Verify you're not using the same object/variable names.
Thanks!

There are really many ways to implement this. Some would be more flexible and customizable than others, but might require more components such as some sort of database. One simple approach would be to introduce a convention and utilize the structure of the file system itself.

```
/ipwb/collections/
├── bar/
│   ├── bar-1.cdxj
│   ├── bar-2.cdxj
│   └── bar-3.cdxj
├── foo/
│   ├── baz/
│   │   └── foo-baz-1.cdxj
│   ├── blah/
│   │   ├── foo-blah-1.cdxj
│   │   ├── foo-blah-2.cdxj
│   │   └── metadata.yaml
│   ├── foo-1.cdxj
│   └── metadata.yaml
└── metadata.yaml
```

Look at the above directory and file organization. With this in place, if the replay server is invoked with the following command:

```
$ ipwb replay /ipwb/collections/
```

The server should recursively read all the CDXJ files that fall under the selected collection name space. For example, when requested for collection `bar`, it should lookup using `bar-1.cdxj`, `bar-2.cdxj`, and `bar-3.cdxj`. When requested for `foo`, it should lookup using `foo-1.cdxj`, `foo-baz-1.cdxj`, `foo-blah-1.cdxj`, and `foo-blah-2.cdxj`. However, when requested for the collection `foo/blah`, it should only lookup using `foo-blah-1.cdxj` and `foo-blah-2.cdxj`. If no collection is specified then it will read all the CDXJ files under /ipwb/collections/ recursively. Additionally, each collection directory (on any nested level) can contain an optional `metadata.yaml` file that will customize various properties of the collection such as a more human friendly for of the collection name, the description of the collection, some inclusion/exclusion patterns to override the default behavior of the replay system for that collection name space. PyWB does similar collection management, but I am not sure if that supports recursive sub-collection feature or just a flat list of collections.

The reason is that the withdrawn route should be as defined in RFC 3107
```
   A BGP speaker can withdraw a previously advertised route (as well as
   the binding between this route and a label) by either (a) advertising
   a new route (and a label) with the same NLRI as the previously
   advertised route, or (b) listing the NLRI of the previously
   advertised route in the Withdrawn Routes field of an Update message.
   The label information carried (as part of NLRI) in the Withdrawn
   Routes field should be set to 0x800000.  (Of course, terminating the
   BGP session also withdraws all the previously advertised routes.)
```

So the announcement should be:
`withdraw route 12.0.0.0/8 next-hop 192.168.1.23 label [ 800000 ]`

Until the family is added to the withdraw command, I do not know if the route is an IPv4 unicast or IPv4 nlri-mpls .. so I can not 'fix' the label for the user.

Changing the syntax of the API before the release of 4.0 is planned but it will be an intrusive change I have not yet started.

The easiest way to do this is to amend the last commit:
~~~console
$ git clone -b "master" git@github.com:cglewis/docker-py.git somewhere
$ cd somewhere
$ git rebase -i HEAD~2
editor opens
change each 'pick' to 'edit'
save the file and quit
$ git commit --amend -s --no-edit
$ git rebase --continue # and repeat the amend for each commit
$ git push -f
~~~

Amending updates the existing PR. You **DO NOT** need to open a new one.

A "pluggable" protocol sounds desirable .. I agree. At least for Crossbar.io, ideally it would work like this:

* CB listens on port 80
* if the first octet is `x7f`, make it a RawSocket protocol
* else, parse the first HTTP request line to get the path
* if the path is configured for WebSocket, hand over to WebSocket protocol
* else, hand over to Twisted Web

Probably also interesting from this perspective: the "origin" field in the WebSocket opening handshake cannot be forged from browser JS, but again becomes snake oil for all language environment able to * implement* WebSocket.

Hi,

short answer: You have to put ipv6 addresses in quotes.

Luqum tries to follow queryString syntax (and provides you with tools to extend its meaning). I have done a quick experiment ([see the gist](https://gist.github.com/alexgarel/92440caec4232c5ad51c569c2d0f465b)) and in ES6 you'll have to quote ipv6, or use backslash to escape columns in it. So that's way luqum will go. (also it would clutter too much the parser to handle ipv6, while not providing any real benefit).

So I'll close this ticket, do not hesitate to reopen it if I missed something or you need more explanations.

Your last comment is the same as this issue: https://github.com/ethereum/web3.py/issues/699, and shows that our documentation needs to be fixed.  Our example has `_from` in the filter arguments, that makes it seem like that is the way to filter for the emanating contract, rather than what it is, an argument specific to the example event.

Because your `LogCluster` event has no `_from` argument, the web3 abi lookup doesnt find an event abi that matches the argument and event name combination.

In other words, the `filter` argument dict is expected to match for event argument.  Based on your event ABI and example code, that might look like this:

```
transfer_filter = eBlocBroker.on('LogCluster', {'filter': {'clusterAddr': cluster_address}})
```

If you just want all logs emanating from that event, just delete the filter parameter.

```
transfer_filter = eBlocBroker.on('LogCluster')
```

@groutr, it would be worth having someone deeply involved in conda to think about whether there are issues with the dependency solver, but I don't *think* there would be.  I.e. the current relationships would all continue just as they are, except that for certain packages people could add additional optional ones, and some of the existing ones could be downgraded to suggestions (which could reveal gaps in other packages' declared dependencies, but if so those were always problems with the other packages, not the one actually being changed here).  I think this approach could actually significantly simplify the dependency solver's job -- if packages had a lean list of genuine core dependencies, instead of the broad kitchen-sink list of everything related to each package that they have now, then finding a valid set of installable packages to fill all required dependencies would get easier. ... another possibility...  Use conda's own magic of rewriting prefixes in text files!  Seems like such an obvious solution.

http://conda.pydata.org/docs/building/meta-yaml.html#text-files-with-prefix-files

We can list whatever text files we want to hard-code full-path in `build:has_prefix_files`.

Just another possibility.
`schannel` is canonicalized, `channel` is a full URL.

Here's the code that conda uses to break cycles:

https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/31130cd80d868b4d5f52db7f89fc53a274e678bf/conda/models/prefix_graph.py#L265-L279

While that's conda 4.5 code, the same algorithm has been used for many years.

Addressed in #115

I think the categorical type can be implemented as an additional type in an external library, given the Cython-API it would be possible.

Some notes on the implementation:

The performance of a categorical type depends very much on the "load-factor" i.e. the ratio of unique types to total number of elements. This influences how to store it in-memory and out-of-core.
I am note yet sure how to store the categories and if we should have a two way lookup. 

Depending on the number of categories, it may be wise to deactivate the shuffle filter. For example if you "only" have < 256 categories, it may or may not make any difference. Anything beyond that depends on both the entropy and the order of the data. For example, if you have 257 categories but the element that maps to integer 256 appears only once in your >> 257 elements dataset, using the shuffle filter should give some nice compression ratios. If on the other hand your coresponding values are sampled uniformly from the closed interval 0-65536 (short/int16) then the shuffle filter is unlikely to give you much advantage.

$ az resource list
```

If there are multiple subscriptions available for a cloud, specify the `--default-subscription` to use when switching to that cloud:
```
$ az cloud update --default-subscription <my-main-subscription>

Switched active cloud to AzureCloud.
Active subscription switched to '<my-main-subscription>'.

This is a great idea. It would be useful to be able to exclude, or specifically set the line ending type for certain files (based on a matching pattern).

As an example, the [Gradle build tool](https://gradle.org/) includes a `wrapper` task, which automatically generates two scripts `gradlew` and `gradlew.bat`. The file `gradlew.bat` is correctly generated with `CRLF` line endings (even on Unix-type operating systems), as it is **only** used on Windows.

The proposed line ending detector/fixer should respect this, and *not* change the line endings of `gradlew.bat` to `LF`, even if the rest of the project is using `LF` as the end-of-line marker. This could work in a similar way to how `trailing-whitespace` by default does *not* trim .md files.

While this is possible with `pre-commit`, access control is probably best achieved through `pre-receive` (though the pre-commit tool *can* be used to implement pre-receive hooks as well!).  Just a small reminder that pre-commit is easy to bypass (either by disabling hooks, or running with `SKIP=...` or `--no-verify`).

>
> Do we actually need Model to implement the entire mapping interface? If
> not, these methods could just as well be named _keys, _items, and so on.
>
This comes do my appreciation of not needing model instances. I think we
should adhere to this going forward.

Objects waste resources and CPU relative to just using dictionaries, so the
purpose of objects implementing the mapping interface is so people can
write functions that work on dict's, and if you happen to have an instance
instead, you can use the same function.


> One annoyance with emulating a mapping is that the keys() method is
> unavoidably invoked by the dict constructor if you attempt naive
> dictionary conversion via dict(instance). Right now, this effectively
> returns a copy of instance._data — unless the model happens to have a
> field called keys (which isn't expressly forbidden), in which case the
> statement fails with TypeError. This occurs because dict doesn't have any
> fallback logic in case keys isn't callable.
>
> Since dict(instance) is not guaranteed to work and doesn't handle
> submodels (there's to_dict() for this reason), I'd actually prefer the
> statement to *never* work to avoid surprises. This can be done by
> defining a dummy class attribute called keys that would be masked by a
> field with the same name, should it exist.
>
I'm fine with an error happening when someone uses a field name `keys`.

Schematics blurs the line between dictionaries and models on purpose and I
want this to stay in the system.

I know I've said it before, but objects are bad. It is my view that we
should push people towards using dicts whenever possible and avoid using
instances of Schematics Models unless necessary. Just pipe data through the
models, get the output you need, and move on to the next thing. For
example, if someone needs `keys` to be part of the data, it's easy enough
to change that by adjusting the name of the key used during serialization
only.

Personal anecdote: The difference between objects and raw functions / data
is significant. In my days of building Brubeck (the web framework), the
difference between using objects or functions for routing turned out to be
roughly 33% gains when using just functions. Python is not a fast language,
but this is significant.

I understand that others in the Schematics community don't have the same
aversion to objects that I have, but it's important to me that this is
continued as a value. It keeps us fast and keeps our code clean and
portable.

Obviously we didn't intend to break backwards compatibility, but it's hard to define exactly what "backwards compatibility" means in general. If you look at where that change came from, I figured it was a bug that "dayfirst" worked in some cases and not in others. I'll admit that, in retrospect, a surprising amount of people were relying on this particular behavior, but to some extent all bugfixes change behavior in a "backwards-incomptible" way for people relying on bugfixes.

As for speed, I'm glad it worked out that way. I don't think most people realize that, under the hood, the parser is quite slow because it has to do a lot of work inferring date formats - which is why we always recommend that you use `strptime` if you _know_ the format of the string. Hopefully we can speed things up somewhat in the 2.7.0 release by providing the ability for people to "cache" the date-inference portion of it, since most of the time if you have a big list of dates, most of them will be in the same format (or in a small number of formats) - no need to do all that work every time.

@johnallen24 Sorry to hear this! Let's try to figure it out. First, can you confirm that this happens when you do `import mxnet`?

Let's make sure MXNet is first completely uninstalled:

      box_preds = [F.flatten(F.transpose(bp(feat), (0, 2, 3, 1)))

      anchors = [F.reshape(ag(feat), shape=(1, -1))
                   for feat, ag in zip(features, self.anchor_generators)]
      cls_preds = F.concat(*cls_preds, dim=1).reshape((0, -1, self.num_classes + 1))
      box_preds = F.concat(*box_preds, dim=1).reshape((0, -1, 4))

@NikolajBjorner 
> What reference counting function should be used on Z3_func_interp objects? Is there a reason the interpretations aren't just stored in the model (i.e. have the Z3_model lifetime be the same as the Z3_func_interp lifetime)?

Oops. I didn't realise `Z3_func_interp` objects were already part of Z3's API. So I should use `Z3_func_interp_inc_ref()` and `Z3_func_interp_dec_ref()`.

However thinking a little more I think forcing the user to reference count `Z3_func_interp` is a bit weird given the current API. The `Z3_add_func_interp()` couples creating a `Z3_func_interp` with adding it to a model. This means that it isn't possible to re-use a `Z3_func_interp` by adding it to a different model. If a `Z3_func_interp` can't be re-used I'm not sure why reference counting is needed because that implies the lifetime of the `Z3_func_interp` is tied to the lifetime of the model.

Thanks for the updates. I've played with it a bit now and have some thoughts.

I don't like bz.login(api_key=XXX). Right now login() is a wrapper for the bugzilla login API call; extending it to handle the api_key parameter to mean 'store this in our token cache' is pulling it in a different direction. I think for starters we should just have Bugzilla(api_key="XXX") be the code entry point for using an API key. That loses the ability to use an api_key outside of Bugzilla() init time but I don't think that's a big concern.

Storing the API key in ~/.config/python-bugzilla/tokencache doesn't really make sense to me, again it seems to be conflating two different paradigms: one is a login token acquired after an explicit login from the user, the other is a long living API password the user has to deliberately create and fetch. For automatic usage of an API key we should mandate the ~/.config/python-bugzilla/bugzillarc route, which the user either fills in manually by hand, and/or we can add a /usr/bin/bugzilla login --save-api-key option or similar, which takes the API key via getpass(), saves it in the RC file, and tells the user "API key saved to ~/.config/..."

In that vein, having a login --api-key option that takes the key on the command line isn't a good idea IMO. I know we have the --password option but that's a bad idea too. It's generally not a good idea to encourage specifying any secret keys/passwords on the command line, since it shows up in ps, bash history, etc. If we want to give an option for testing a one off API key then we should use an environment variable, but not sure how important that use case is.

So the change order should be roughly:

1) Add Bugzilla(api_key=X), make it work, add test case and example. api_key is transient and not saved to disk anywhere.
2) Add bugzillarc support for api_key
3) Consider a bugzilla login --save-api-key convenience option. This might need some rework of the bugzillarc code, to make it it's own file/API object that bin/bugzilla interacts with. That or add a convenience function like Bugzilla.save_api_key(...). Not sure, I'd need to play with it.

Another thing to investigate is how this fails when the key is wrong/revoked, I think we would want to amend the error to point to the bugzillarc file if that's where the key came from.

My strong feeling toward the ```_proxyObject``` classes is that it should be so lightweight that we don't miss it not being passed around with the actual ```GangaObject```. We always have to make one when constructing an ```GangaObject``` from 'in front' of the Proxy layer anyway so this is no more of less expensive with this PR.
The only potential performance loss is due to possible multiple decelerations of ```_proxyClass``` objects.

We could be intelligent and store a dictionary of proxied classes (```_proxyClass```) and perform a dictionary lookup whenever we need one, but I suspect that there would be little performance loss anywhere as I don't know of anywhere in the code that these are constructed many, many times rather than in ```exportToGPI```.

between frames is the coordinate best-fit RMSD between those frames.
Clustering on RMSD to a single reference could be useful in some cases, but
since RMSD to a single reference is "fuzzier" the higher the value is you
can get wildly different structures placed into the same cluster.

The removal of with is based on the same idea as cl/191121016 (my cl/190950450 just expands the scope of the SWIG change).  

When a C function with a param "TF_Status* status" is wrapped by SWIG, the generated wrapper will correctly generate a TF_NewStatus(), and pass it into the C function. On exit of the C function, the wrapper will check and raise the appropriate error.
