It's used by the ``tools/mkdoc.py`` script which is used by all of my projects that use pybind11. It would be nice to document it at some point.

This script reads a bunch of C++ header files and invokes Clang via libclang (in parallel) to generate an AST and extract all the comments. 

Example:

```cpp
/*
  This file contains docstrings for the Python bindings.
  Do not edit! These were automatically extracted by mkdoc.py
 */

#define __EXPAND(x)                                      x
#define __COUNT(_1, _2, _3, _4, _5, _6, _7, COUNT, ...)  COUNT
#define __VA_SIZE(...)                                   __EXPAND(__COUNT(__VA_ARGS__, 7, 6, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1))
#define __CAT1(a, b)                                     a ## b
#define __CAT2(a, b)                                     __CAT1(a, b)
#define __DOC1(n1)                                       __doc_##n1
#define __DOC2(n1, n2)                                   __doc_##n1##_##n2
#define __DOC3(n1, n2, n3)                               __doc_##n1##_##n2##_##n3
#define __DOC4(n1, n2, n3, n4)                           __doc_##n1##_##n2##_##n3##_##n4
#define __DOC5(n1, n2, n3, n4, n5)                       __doc_##n1##_##n2##_##n3##_##n4##_##n5
#define __DOC6(n1, n2, n3, n4, n5, n6)                   __doc_##n1##_##n2##_##n3##_##n4##_##n5##_##n6
#define __DOC7(n1, n2, n3, n4, n5, n6, n7)               __doc_##n1##_##n2##_##n3##_##n4##_##n5##_##n6##_##n7
#define DOC(...)                                         __EXPAND(__EXPAND(__CAT2(__DOC, __VA_SIZE(__VA_ARGS__)))(__VA_ARGS__))

#if defined(__GNUG__)
#pragma GCC diagnostic push
#pragma GCC diagnostic ignored "-Wunused-variable"
#endif

static const char *__doc_mitsuba_AtomicFloat =
R"doc(Atomic floating point data type

The class implements an an atomic floating point data type (which is
not possible with the existing overloads provided by ``std::atomic``).
It internally casts floating point values to an integer storage format
and uses atomic integer compare and exchange operations to perform
changes.)doc";

static const char *__doc_mitsuba_AtomicFloat_AtomicFloat = R"doc(Initialize the AtomicFloat with a given floating point value)doc";

static const char *__doc_mitsuba_AtomicFloat_operator_assign = R"doc(Overwrite the AtomicFloat with a floating point value)doc";

static const char *__doc_mitsuba_AtomicFloat_operator_iadd = R"doc(Atomically add a floating point value)doc";

static const char *__doc_mitsuba_AtomicFloat_operator_idiv = R"doc(Atomically divide by a floating point value)doc";

static const char *__doc_mitsuba_AtomicFloat_operator_imul = R"doc(Atomically multiply by a floating point value)doc";

static const char *__doc_mitsuba_AtomicFloat_operator_isub = R"doc(Atomically subtract a floating point value)doc";
```

In the Python bindings, these can then be referenced using the overloaded macro:

```cpp
#include <mitsuba/core/atomic.h>
#include "python.h" // includes #define D(...) DOC(mitsuba, __VA_ARGS__)

MTS_PY_EXPORT(atomic) {
    py::class_<AtomicFloat<>>(m, "AtomicFloat", D(AtomicFloat))
        .def(py::init<Float>(), D(AtomicFloat, AtomicFloat))
        .def(py::self += Float(), D(AtomicFloat, operator, iadd))
        .def(py::self -= Float(), D(AtomicFloat, operator, imul))
        .def(py::self *= Float(), D(AtomicFloat, operator, imul))
        .def(py::self /= Float(), D(AtomicFloat, operator, idiv))
        .def("__float__", [](const AtomicFloat<> &af) { return (Float) af; },
             D(AtomicFloat, operator, T0));
}```

The reason I don't like the proposed workaround is that it expects the plugin to crawl the certificates and determine which certificates have been recently renewed. Why do this when the renew process already knows? It also overloads restart. To top it all off, because this integration would be at the plugin level, some plugins might not implement this features, some might not allow for it to be turned off, or some might allow it to be optional but don't have a convention for what the flag would be called.

Is there a reason that there is not just a flag that will cause renew to call deploy_cert on all renewed certs?

This would avoid breaking expectations for existing installs since they will not be using the flag. This would also avoid needing to add new methods to the plugin interface. Since this flag could be saved in the renewal information, it would be per certificate instead of system wide. Also, all existing plugins would able to take advantage of the feature without modification.

Right, currently if you do

```python
import ray
ray.init()

x = ray.put(1)
y = ray.put(x)

x2 = ray.get(y)
```

Then `x` and `x2` are the same, but `x` and `y` are different.

```python
assert x2 == x
assert x != y
```

However, as you suggest, we may want to throw an exception if someone calls `ray.put` on an object ID because it's probably being done on accident.

Note that we currently *disallow* returning an object ID from a remote function, for example,

```python
@ray.remote
def f():
  return 1

@ray.remote
def g():
  return f.remote()
```

Calling `g.remote()` fails with

```
Remote function __main__.g failed with:

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Users/rkn/Workspace/ray/python/ray/worker.py", line 1637, in process_task
    store_outputs_in_objstore(return_object_ids, outputs, worker)
  File "/Users/rkn/Workspace/ray/python/ray/worker.py", line 1985, in store_outputs_in_objstore
    raise Exception("This remote function returned an ObjectID as its {}th return value. This is not allowed.".format(i))
Exception: This remote function returned an ObjectID as its 0th return value. This is not allowed.
```

If you really want to do this, then you can define `g` as follows.

```python
@ray.remote
def g():
  return [f.remote()]
```

A different API I'd propose here is to use context managers for this:

Usage with the `with` statement:

```python
@ray.remote
def f(something):
    # Do something

with ray.job(name="jobname"):
    x = f.remote(1)
    # Do other things
```

Context managers can also be used without the `with` statement if needed, see the python documentation: https://docs.python.org/2/reference/datamodel.html#context-managers

The semantics here would be that there is one default job per driver and you can create custom ones that inherit all objects and function definitions from that default job. It is not allowed to pass objects between these inner jobs, so after the context manager is closed on the driver and all tasks from that job have finished, things can be cleaned up.

I've had rude or impolite discussions before, this is clearly not one them. So no worries.

Let me give you some backstory to explain my point of view.

I've been using Chatty for a long time (before that: IRC client + Livestreamer), it's the only app I use for Twitch besides the occasional visit of their website. At some point I took in interest in turning this into a proper (self-contained) macOS app so I don't have to perform 3rd party software installations to run it. The sole purpose is convenience (and exercise), plain and simple. My main goals are
- macOS app that runs out-of-the-box on my Mac
- ability to view streams with VLC
- stay as close to vanilla-Chatty as possible (I don't speak Java)

For that reason
- it comes bundled with Java and Streamlink because I want it that way.
- I do not bundle Python because that comes with the OS.
- I refrain from interfering with the sourcecode as much as possible. That includes cosmetic changes like renaming all occurences of "Livestreamer" to "Streamlink".

I've been doing this for a target audience of one: me. But my thinking is "since I've spent some time doing this, why not put it out there, maybe someone else finds it useful" (also: Opensource. Take something and give something back.). 
So right now I'm only catering to my own needs, therefore most of your concerns in regards to global installations, configs and such, forcing VLC etc. are non-issues for me.

Things I haven't adressed yet:
- I do not see a license issue in regards to binary redistribution once I include the proper license info - that is an oversight on my part. I should also look that up for the dependencies that Streamlink pulls in. (worst case: pull the plug altogether)
- I wasn't aware that "issues" were disabled and didn't check it before submitting the PR. My mistake.
- Squashing the commit history: I'm still trying stuff out and am surely not aware of all the best practices that one should stick too. Since I don't care about the change history and didn't think about all the possible angles, I did it the way I did. I do see your point though, so I'll likely reset and start fresh some time.

So what is my conclusion... well, I'm not here to force myself onto you, nor am I currently willing to put work into things that I don't need right now, so my advice is to discard my PR.

I think for one thing the abstraction is wrong - tests are not user-facing code, they are part of development and deployment. They don't serve to benefit the *user* at all, except indirectly in that it might aid the developers to diagnose or fix a problem. Some of the problems of shipping the tests are basically problems mainly because they are burdens on the user (albeit possibly minor) with no benefit for the user.

Some downsides I see:

1. To the extent that your tests are intended for use by the users, they become part of the public API, meaning changes to the tests and test organization might be a backwards-incompatible change (though probably most people aren't actually relying on the tests for anything).
2. As you add more data, the fact that the tests are there at all is an increasing burden - you may end up shipping 100 MB of images, videos, etc for your tests, when the library/app itself is only 10kb.
3. You either end up introducing unnecessary runtime dependencies on libraries you only need for tests (e.g. pytest, freezegun, etc), or you're shipping something where the tests don't even work in a standard installation out of the box *anyway*.

Most of these are not huge show-stoppers in most situations, but the benefit you get (users can run tests even from a wheel install) is not really worth it. As long as the tests are included in the source distribution, users who really want to run the tests can do so pretty easily, though I think this won't be something that is of general interest anyway, since most of the time a user has problems with something specific that your library is failing at (i.e. they already *have* a failing test case). Unit tests are more useful for saying, "is this library failing, and if so, how?" As long as the tests have already been run upstream, the only reason to run them again once you have them is to check that they work on your particular platform. That's something that is useful for people running package managers (e.g. debian, arch linux), but they're probably building from source or repo anyway.

Briefly in the meantime:

> I can't seem to find any examples explaining why this is the case.

It actually hadn't occurred to me that this part might be controversial :-). Obviously side-by-side comparison is great, but even twisted and asyncio's biggest fans will agree that approachability is not their strongest suit.

> Perhaps you could take a couple use cases and then implement them in both trio and asyncio (and Twisted?), and then explain why the trio implementation is simpler, or better, etc.

[The first part of this blog post is exactly that](https://vorpus.org/blog/some-thoughts-on-asynchronous-api-design-in-a-post-asyncawait-world/); it uses curio instead of trio for the comparison, but trio is basically designed as a next-generation curio.

I've also been thinking that "happy eyeballs" would be a good example for another blog post, since it's a small but real and decidedly non-trivial problem where I can't be accused of cherry picking. (Trio's implementation is in #145; twisted's is in their `HostnameEndpoint`, though their current version is generally agreed to be very hard to understand so there's also a branch where glyph is working on rewriting it. AFAIK no one has yet implemented this for asyncio: https://github.com/python/asyncio/issues/86)

> Also, it would be good to be honest. What does someone give up by choosing trio over asyncio.

Mainly the community and library ecosystem (at least for now). Though this is also what you give up choosing asyncio over twisted :-).

> Finally, what's involved in transitioning to and from trio? Wholesale rewrite? Can one transition incrementally, etc?

Currently there isn't any particular interoperability story, though see #171 for some notes on how we could fix that.

As an extra data point, in PyTorch we model optional inputs using an `Undefined` operator, which produces an "undefined tensor", much like null.

On the other hand, NULL was Tony Hoare's billion dollar mistake ;)

Shall we add some attribute_parser during operator registration, like typedef std::function<bool(const NodeProto)> AttributeParser, so that in operator declaration, all attributes are able to be checked in detail.

You may notice that two more fields are added in AttributeProto to cover the case that an attribute is referring to a function attribute declaration (the data stored in parent node's AttributeProto actually).
 optional bool is_ref = 21;          // if it's true, it means it's referring parent node's (referring to a function) attribute value.
  optional string ref_attr_name = 22; // if is_ref is true, ref_attr_name is the attribute name in parent function.

After more discussion with @yuanbyu  the bool is_ref looks to be redundant and having ref_attr_name is good enough, since if the ref_attr_name is empty, we can know that the attribute contains real data not a reference without checking another separate flag.
